coredata.dc:title: Difference between ammonia and urea on nitrogen doping of graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Yanyun
Yan, Luting
Xu, Mingyuan
Li, Yuting
Song, Xiaodong
Yin, Lan
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) with prime fluorescence properties were synthesized through a simple one-pot hydrothermal method by using citric acid as carbon source and ammonia (A-GQDs) or urea (U-GQDs) as nitrogen source. Both A-GQDs and U-GQDs exhibited a graphene-like structure, but their average particle size is about ten times different. A-GQDs exhibited an evident excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) behavior. By contrast, the emission of U-GQDs can be divided into two regions with the development of excitation wavelength: a wavelength-independent emission peak at approximately 540 nm (green) with the excitation wavelength from 400 nm to 500 nm and an excitation-dependent emission peak with the excitation wavelength below 400 nm. Ammonia was introduced into citric acid polycondensator in the form of five membered ring and urea in the form of seven membered ring, respectively, which further affected the subsequent crystallization and surface functionalization. At this time, the structural instability of the nitrogen-containing doped ring itself, coupled with the progress of carbonization, mostly forms a stable nitrogen-containing ring structure, which is likely to be pyrrolic nitrogen or graphitized nitrogen. Therefore, the differences in particle size and nitrogen-containing doped rings partially between A-GQDs and U-GQDs lead to the differences in optical properties. Finally, U-GQDs with high quantum yield (QY) of 54 % were obtained at the molar ratio of 9:10 (citric acid/urea) by optimizing the reaction conditions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
Photoluminescence intensity
High quantum yield
Graphene skeleton
Luminescence mechanism

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) and (B) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of A-GQDs and U-GQDs (insets of (A) and (B) represent the histogram of A-GQDs and U-GQDs diameter distributions); (C) FT-IR spectra and (D) Raman spectra (λex = 425 nm) of A-GQDs and U-GQDs.
(A) XPS survey spectrum of A-GQDs and U-GQDs; High-resolution peak-fitting XPS spectra of (C) C 1s and (D) N 1s region for A-GQDs and U-GQDs.
Schematic diagram and detailed structure of the feasible formation process for the A-GQDs and U-GQDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra, (B) PL spectra and (C) Relative quantum yield (QY) of A-GQDs at the same reaction time (10 h) and a series of material ratios (1:5; 1:10; 1:12.5; 1:15, g: mL). (D) UV–vis absorption spectra, (E) PL spectra and (F) QY of A-GQDs at the identical material ratios (1:10/g/mL) and varied reaction time (6 h; 8 h; 10 h; 12 h; 14 h). (G) UV–vis absorption spectra, (H) PL spectra and (I) QY of U-GQDs at the same reaction time (4 h) and different molar ratios changed from 1:10 to 9:10 (mol : mol). (J) UV–vis absorption spectra, (K) PL spectra and (L) QY of U-GQDs at the identical material ratios (9:10/mol/mol) and varied reaction time (2 h; 4 h; 6 h; 8 h; 10 h).

(A) and (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of A-GQDs and U-GQDs (black line) and PL emission spectra of A-GQDs and U-GQDs (green line). (C) and (D) the normalized PL intensity of the A-GQDs and U-GQDs under different excitation wavelengths. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

(A) PL spectra of A-GQDs with different concentrations (0.0125; 0.025; 0.05 mg/mL; from 0.1 mg/mL to 1.0 mg/mL). Variations in (B) optimum PL intensity and (C) prime PL emission peak position of A-GQDs with different concentrations.

(A) PL spectra of U-GQDs with different concentrations (0.0125; 0.025; 0.05 mg/mL; from 0.1 mg/mL to 1.0 mg/mL). Variations in (B) optimum PL intensity and (C) prime PL emission peak position of U-GQDs with different concentrations.
Elemental compositions of A-GQDs and U-GQDs as determined via XPS.
XPS data analyses of the C 1s spectra of the A-GQDs and U-GQDs.
XPS data analyses of the N 1s spectra of the A-GQDs and U-GQDs.
PL intensity and QY of GQDs prepared at different carbon sources.
PL intensity and QY of N-GQDs synthesized using all kinds of nitrogen sources (CA is the carbon source).
PL intensity and QY of N-GQDs prepared at varied reaction solvents.

head.ce:title: Difference between ammonia and urea on nitrogen doping of graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Science, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) with prime fluorescence properties were synthesized through a simple one-pot hydrothermal method by using citric acid as carbon source and ammonia (A-GQDs) or urea (U-GQDs) as nitrogen source. Both A-GQDs and U-GQDs exhibited a graphene-like structure, but their average particle size is about ten times different. A-GQDs exhibited an evident excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) behavior. By contrast, the emission of U-GQDs can be divided into two regions with the development of excitation wavelength: a wavelength-independent emission peak at approximately 540 nm (green) with the excitation wavelength from 400 nm to 500 nm and an excitation-dependent emission peak with the excitation wavelength below 400 nm. Ammonia was introduced into citric acid polycondensator in the form of five membered ring and urea in the form of seven membered ring, respectively, which further affected the subsequent crystallization and surface functionalization. At this time, the structural instability of the nitrogen-containing doped ring itself, coupled with the progress of carbonization, mostly forms a stable nitrogen-containing ring structure, which is likely to be pyrrolic nitrogen or graphitized nitrogen. Therefore, the differences in particle size and nitrogen-containing doped rings partially between A-GQDs and U-GQDs lead to the differences in optical properties. Finally, U-GQDs with high quantum yield (QY) of 54 % were obtained at the molar ratio of 9:10 (citric acid/urea) by optimizing the reaction conditions.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) with high photoluminescence (PL) intensity and relative quantum yield (QY) have attracted extensive and significant research attention [1–10]. Compared with top-down routes [11–15], the bottom-up routes have evident advantages in adjusting the composition and physical properties of GQDs through the careful selection of diversified organic precursors and carbonization conditions [16–19]. Two contradictory PL behavior, namely, excitation-independent and excitation-dependent PL emissions, are found in the obtained GQDs regardless of the approach used. GQDs with excitation-independent PL properties exhibit a unique and uniform PL emission in a certain wavelength range [20–22]. Therefore, GQDs with different fluorescent colors can be used at various stages in accordance with the PL emission requirement at different wavebands. As an easily adjustable PL emission that only needs to vary the excitation wavelength without changing the structures of GQDs, excitation-dependent PL emission is extremely advantageous for their wide applications. Pan et al. reported a top-down hydrothermal approach for the cutting of graphene sheets into surface-functionalized GQDs that exhibit an excitation-dependent PL behavior [23]. He et al. developed amphiphilic GQDs by grafting hydrophobic hexylamine on hydrophilic GQDs (GQDs-COOH) through the catalyzed formation of amide bonds in aqueous medium. The amphiphilic GQDs indicated excitation-independent emission because of their uniform structure [24]. Huang et al. presented a combination of a bottom-up synthetic and a solvent-induced interface separation process, where oxygen-rich products with excitation-wavelength dependence and highly crystalline products with excitation-wavelength independence are separated [25]. Elemental doping is an effective method used to change the structure and tune the optical and electrical properties of GQDs. GQDs have shown improved PL and QY through elemental doping [26]. Fan et al. reported water soluble and surface-unmodified GQDs with a QY of 5.5 % [27]. Zhang et al. synthesized highly fluorescent water-soluble nitrogen-doped carbon dots through a simple one-pot hydrothermal synthesis by using urea as nitrogen source with a QY as high as 42.2 % [28]. Cai et al. prepared N-GQDs through a simple one-step method by using citric acid (CA) as carbon source and ammonia as nitrogen source, which exhibited a blue emission with a QY of 18.6 % [29]. GQDs prepared through various methods have demonstrated different properties in terms of size, surface oxidation degree, and surface functionalization. The effects of each factor on certain properties can be separately investigated through characterization analysis. Deep insights into studying the synthesis mechanism of GQDs and understanding the origin of PL emission related to excitation wavelength, concentration, and high QY are highly instructive for modulating their luminescence, which is relatively beneficial for their wide potential applications.

In this study, N-GQDs were synthesized through a facile one-step hydrothermal synthesis by using CA as carbon source and ammonia (A-GQDs) or urea (U-GQDs) as N source, respectively. Many efforts to increase QY particularly focused on the synthesis of highly fluorescent N-GQDs, and high-quality N-GQDs were prepared by optimizing the reaction conditions, such as reaction time and the ratio of reactants. The as-obtained A-GQDs and U-GQDs possessed high PL (QYs of 45.4 % and 54 %, respectively). The PL behavior of A-GQDs showed a remarkable redshift with the increase in excitation wavelength. The PL behavior of U-GQDs in the two excitation wavelength regions showed an excitation-dependent and an excitation-independent PL emission, respectively. The formation process and luminescence mechanism of N-GQDs were investigated. A-GQDs and U-GQDs showed remarkable optical properties, implying their considerable potential for application in ion detection and photovoltaic devices [30–38].

All chemicals used in this work were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Citric acid (CA), ammonia, urea, Tris (hydroxymethyl) methyl aminomethane (Tris-HMA), graphene oxide (GO), glucose, glutamic acid, starsh, ethanolamine, thiourea, NaOH, N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF), tert-Butanol and dialysis bag (1000 Da, molecular weight cut off) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Company or Beijing Chemical Reagent Company. Deionized water was used for aqueous solution preparation in all of the experiments.

A-GQDs: N-GQDs were prepared using a standard hydrothermal method with minor modifications in accordance with previously reported literature [39]. CA (1 g) and ammonia solution (10 mL) were mixed in a glassware and formed a homogeneous and transparent solution under vigorous stirring. The mixture was then transferred into a 25 mL Teflon- lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 10 h. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was placed in the dark overnight. The supernatant was collected by centrifuging at 8000 rpm for 10 min. The obtained crude product was filtered with 220 nm mixed cellulose membrane (PES membrane) to remove the remaining residue. The above solution was placed in a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off value of 1000 Da) by using deionized water as the dialysis solution under magnetic stirring, and the water was changed every 7 h until full dialysis for 3 days. The obtained light-yellow N-GQD solution was freeze-dried on a vacuum freeze dryer for 2 days. Some powders were redispersed in water to make a solution with certain concentration, and the solution was stored in the dark at 4 °C for later experiments. A-GQDs were used to denote the GQDs that were prepared using ammonia.

U-GQDs: N-GQDs were synthesized with some modifications according to the previous procedure [40]. CA (0.21 g) and urea (0.6 g) were dissolved in 5 mL water, and stirred to form a clear solution. The solution was subsequently transferred into a 25 mL Teflon lined stainless autoclave. The sealed autoclave was heated to 180 °C in an electric thermostatic-drying oven and kept for additional 4 h. After cooling down to room temperature naturally, the supernatant was gathered by centrifuging at 8000 rpm for 10 min. The solution was filtered with 220 nm nanoporous inorganic filter membrane and dialyzed every 7 h to change the water for 3 days to eliminate all residual impurities. The blackish-green N-GQDs powders were obtain by vacuum freeze drying for 48 h. Some powders were redispersed in the water. The U-GQDs were used to denote the GQDs that were prepared using urea. The pure U-GQDs solution was stored in the dark environment at 4 °C for subsequent testing.

The topographic morphology and size of the N-GQDs were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Chemical structures were analyzed by fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), Raman and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorption and PL spectra of A-GQDs and U-GQDs were investigated, respectively.

As is known to all, quinine sulfate can emit blue fluorescence dissolved in 0.10 m sulfuric acid solution under excitation wavelength at 360 nm, with a quantum yield (QY) of 54 % [12,20,26,29,41]. The QY of the GQDs is calculated using quinine sulfate as a reference using the following formula:
Q Y =
Q Y
s t d
×
I
I
s t d
×
A
s t d
A
×
n 2
n
s t d
2
Where QY is the quantum yield; I is measured integrated emission intensity; A is absorbance of solution at 360 nm; n is refractive index of solvent (1.33 for water); the subscript std is reference standard sample, i.e. quinine sulfate. In order to eliminate the multiple absorption effect, the absorbance of GQDs solution and quinine sulfate standard solution at 360 nm excitation wavelength should preferably be kept below 0.05.

Ammonia and urea were chosen to prepare high luminescent N-GQDs because they can be used as N source for nitrogen doping processes. Fig. 1 A and B show the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the two types of GQDs produced in this work. The statistical particle size distributions of A-GQDs and U-GQDs are 16–20.5 and 1.4–1.8 nm, with average diameters of 18.2 ± 0.8 and 1.6 ± 0.1 nm, respectively. A-GQDs and U-GQDs possess a quasi-spherical shape [42]. The significant difference in particle size between A-GQDs and U-GQDs is likely due to condensation degree of CA with diverse nitrogen sources. Further, the disparities of bonding compositions and functional groups of the two types of GQDs were investigated through Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Fig. 1C). A-GQDs and U-GQDs display strong and broad absorption bands at 3425 and 3150 cm−1, which are ascribed to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH, respectively. This finding indicate that these functional groups improve the hydrophilicity and stability of GQDs in aqueous solution [28]. The bands at 1670 and 1580 cm−1 belong to the stretching vibrations of CO and CN in the FTIR spectra of A-GQDs, respectively [41,43]. The CN peak of U-GQDs becomes weak, whereas the CO peak becomes strong and broad. This condition suggests that more carbon is forming rings, thereby resulting in the formation of crystalline graphene. For the FTIR spectra of CA, the bands at 1727 and 1635 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibration of carboxyl and carbonyl, respectively. The typical CO peak at 1727 cm−1 shifts to 1670 cm−1 owing to nitrogen doping. Two newly emerging bands at 3150 and 1580 cm−1 are ascribed to the NH and CN stretching vibrations, respectively [44]. This finding indicate that N atoms are doped in the framework of GQDs, and amide and amine groups are found on the surface of GQDs. In addition to oxygen-containing functional groups, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and carbonyl groups, nitrogen-containing functional groups, such as amino and amide groups, are found on the surface of GQDs. The main differences between A-GQDs and U-GQDs are the type of ring formation and the content of surface oxygen and nitrogen-related groups along their surfaces. Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 1D) was used to characterize the microstructure of A-GQDs and U-GQDs. A-GQDs and U-GQDs showed a typical Raman spectrum of GQDs with broad peaks. In additional, they showed a disordered (D) band at 1285 cm−1, which is related to the presence of sp3 defects, and a crystalline (G) band at 1582 cm−1, which is related to the in-plane vibration of sp2 carbon [40,45,46]. The intensity ratio (ID/IG) of these characteristic bands can be used to correlate the structural properties of carbon. The ID/IG values of U-GQDs (0.769) are lower than that of A-GQDs (0.782), indicating that U-GQDs possess higher crystal structure. The results can be correlated with FTIR.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) characterization was conducted to determine the elemental compositions and the surface state of A-GQDs and U-GQDs. The peaks of A-GQDs and U-GQDs at 285, 400, and 532 eV for C1 s, N1 s, and O1 s appeared in the full XPS spectra [47], as illustrated in Fig. 2 A. This finding confirmed the successful nitrogen doping of GQDs through simple one-pot hydrothermal synthesis. Accordingly, the relative atomic percentages (%) were 60.21/29.11/10.68 (C/O/N) for A-GQDs and 55.75/31.42/12.83 (C/O/N) for U-GQDs, respectively (Table 1 ). The peak intensity ratios of N1 s/C1 s (RN/C) in A-GQDs and U-GQDs calculated from the full survey XPS spectra were 0.18 and 0.23, respectively [48,49]. This finding indicated the N doping degree of the two types of GQDs. The high-resolution C1 s XPS spectra of A-GQDs and U-GQDs are shown in Fig. 2B and Table 2 . The spectra can be fitted into three Gaussian peaks at 284.74 (49.15 %), 285.80 (20.00 %), and 288.43 eV (30.85 %) in the A-GQDs, which correspond to the sp2 carbon (CC/CC) in graphene, sp3 carbon (CO and CN), and CO in the carboxyl group, respectively. U-GQDs showed a dominant sp2 C peak at 284.76 with a fraction of 53.59 %, sp3 carbon peak at 287.47 eV (29.43 %), and a carbonyl peak at 288.60 eV (16.98 %). The sp2 C bonding composition of U-GQDs (53.59 %) is higher than that of A-GQDs (49.15 %). which also corresponds to the results of XRD (in Figure S2) and Raman. This finding indicates that the carbon may be bound in the form of rings or double bonds in the structure of U-GQDs, revealing the high graphitization [50,51]. The sum of C and O components of A-GQDs and the sum of the components of sp3 C and oxidized C-containing oxygen functional groups are higher than that of U-GQDs. In particular, A-GQDs contain many oxygen-containing functional groups on their surface, and U-GQDs possess a graphene-like crystal structure. The relative intensity ratio of sp3 C and sp2 C of U-GQDs is 0.55, which is greater than that of A-GQDs at 0.41 because more N atoms enter the graphene structure. As shown in Fig. 2C and Table 3 , two peaks at 399.75 (50.46 %) and 401.15 eV (49.54 %) are found in the N 1s spectrum of A-GQDs, which are attributed to the pyrrolic N (C-N-C) and graphitic N or NH bands, respectively. The spectrum of U-GQDs can be fitted with two Gaussian peaks at 399.91 and 401.28 eV that correspond to the fraction of 49.88 % and 50.12 %, respectively. The presence of pyrrolic N indicates that the N bond interacts with C bonds in a five-membered ring formed by dehydration between adjacent carboxyl and amino groups. Graphite N originates from the bonding of an N atom with three adjacent C atoms [20,48]. At this point, the nitrogen in A-GQDs and U-GQDs is more in the form of pyrrolic nitrogen or graphitized nitrogen. In detail, CA molecules undergo polycondensation through intermolecular dehydration to form a graphene skeleton at first. As a carbon source, citric acid is also an acidic substance, which can ionize H+ in the solution. At the same time, ammonia can directly provide OH−, which will accelerate the reaction rate and be more conducive to the progress of the polycondensation reaction, which results in A-GQDs with large particle size. Urea needs to be hydrolyzed first to indirectly provide OH−, thus, the reaction rate of the polycondensation slows down. Therefore, U-GQDs introduces more N in the structure to show a stronger crystal structure. The major differences between A-GQDs and U-GQDs are the bonding mode and the content of surface oxygen-related groups.

A schematic diagram for growing N-GQDs through the facile one-step hydrothermal synthesis of CA (carbon source) and ammonia or urea (nitrogen source) is proposed on the basis of experimental results and comprehensive understanding, as illustrated in Fig. 3 . The formation of N-GQDs mainly involves the three following steps [48,52,53]. First, CA molecules form graphene framework through intermolecular dehydration. Second, amides are then formed by dehydration and condensation of COOH (in carbon source) and NH2 (in nitrogen source) under the action of nitrogen source. In the third step, the amide can continue to form pyrrolic N through intramolecular dehydration with adjacent carboxyl groups. More N enter the graphene skeleton with continuous reaction, and the carbonized structure of the N-GQDs is formed. The two kinds of N-GQDs prepared on the structure show a carbon core with sp2 carbon forming a network structure. However, A-GQDs and U-GQDs possess slightly different nitrogen-containing heterocyclic structures. A-GQDs contains an amino group due to the ammonia structure, and the N-doped ring is mainly five-membered ring. However, U-GQDs, in addition to the ammonia obtained by hydrolysis, most of the remaining unhydrolyzed urea molecules have the structure of a diamide, and the N-doped ring is mostly seven-membered ring. At this time, the structural instability of the nitrogen-containing doped ring itself, coupled with the progress of carbonization, mostly forms a stable nitrogen-containing ring structure, which is likely to be pyrrolic nitrogen or graphitized nitrogen. This can also be supported by the results of XPS high-resolution N1 s Gaussian fitting peaks. Therefore, A-GQDs and U-GQDs show differences in particle size and nitrogen-containing doped rings, which in turn shows differences in structure and optical properties.

As shown in the UV–vis spectrum in Fig. 5 A, A-GQDs exhibited two deep UV absorption peaks centered at 230 and 335 nm, which are assigned to the π→π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains and n→π* transitions between energy levels from the surface states arising from the amino, carboxyl, and amide functional groups [20]. As shown in the UV–vis spectrum in Fig. 5B, U-GQDs showed two absorption bands at 316 and 346 nm, which are redshifted compared with the absorption bands of A-GQDs. The two absorption peaks indicate the existence of conjugate structure in N-GQDs. A strong blue emission from A-GQDs and U-GQDs was observed at 442 and 457 nm under 365 nm excitation, respectively. U-GQDs exhibited a redshift of 15 nm at the PL emission peak compared with A-GQDs, which may be related to the surface states of N-GQDs. The normalized PL intensity of A-GQDs and U-GQDs was excitation wavelength (λex)–dependent, as shown in Fig. 5C and 5D. With the increase in excitation wavelength from 350 nm to 450 nm, the PL peak of A-GQDs shifted to long wavelengths with a maximum peak at 510 nm when excited at 430 nm (Fig. 5C). Such excitation-dependent PL behavior is related to the surface states of A-GQDs to a large extent. A-GQDs possess disordered structure, large size, attached chemical functionalities, nitrogen doping and defects. The effect of size on the quantum field might lead to diverse excited states of GQDs [58,59]. Unlike most previously reported luminescent GQDs, the emission evolution of U-GQDs with excitation wavelength can be separated into two regions: a wavelength-independent emission peak at approximately 540 nm (green) with the excitation wavelength from 400 nm to 500 nm and an excitation-dependent emission peak with the excitation wavelength below 400 nm (Fig. 5D). The PL of GQDs is essentially derived from the quantum confinement of conjugated π electrons in the sp2 carbon network, and can be sensitively tuned by its size, edge configuration, shape, attached chemical functionalities, heteroatom doping and defects. The observed PL emission in the two excitation wavelength regions indicates emissive traps with relatively uniform surface state [28]. This condition is related to the small size and compact size distribution of U-GQDs. The characteristics of UV–vis and PL spectra indicate that N-GQDs may have different emission origins. The change in the UV absorption peak and PL change with the excitation wavelength of A-GQDs and U-GQDs can be attributed to the size-dependent quantum confinement effect, and the energy gap dominated by surface trap states of modified functional groups. A-GQDs and U-GQDs show differences in structure, resulting in different nitrogen-containing doped rings formed, which in turn shows differences in structure and optical properties.

Various N-GQD solutions at different concentrations were prepared to investigate their PL properties, and their PL spectra was systematically studied [50]. As shown in the 3D image (Fig. 6 A), the PL intensity, optimal concentration, and emission wavelength of A-GQDs at 365 nm excitation wavelength were different with the change (0.0125; 0.025; 0.05 mg/mL; from 0.1 mg/mL to 1.0 mg/mL) in concentration, indicating that the GQDs are related to the excitation wavelength and concentration [20,41]. The intensity of emission peak demonstrated a rise-and-fall trend in the PL intensity with the increase in concentration, and the optimum concentration was 0.5 mg/mL, as shown in Fig. 6B. The effect of the concentration of A-GQDs on fluorescence emission peak can be divided into two regions. The emission peak position shows no evident shift and remains at 445 nm, and the peak intensity first increases, gradually decreases, and reaches its maximum at the concentration of 0–0.3 mg/mL. The emission peak is located at 450 nm, and the peak intensity decreases with the darkening of the solution color at the range of 0.4–1 mg/mL (Fig. 6C). Fig. 7 A shows the variation of the optical properties of U-GQDs with concentration at 365 nm excitation wavelength, as clearly shown in the 3D image. Fig. 7B shows the parabolic trend of emission peak intensity with increasing concentration, and the optimum concentration is 0.5 mg/mL. As shown in Fig. 7C, the effect of the concentration of U-GQDs on the fluorescence emission peak is different from that of A-GQDs, and the fluorescence emission peak is biased toward redshift with the increase in concentration. The peak shifts gradually to the long wavelength when the concentration is 0–0.7 mg/mL, and the peak intensity first increases, decreases, and reaches its maximum at 0.5 mg/mL. At the concentration of 0.7 mg/mL, the emission peak is located at the longest wavelength of 460 nm. The peak intensity decreases with the darkening of solution color when the concentration is higher than 0.7 mg/mL. Combining with the atypical shift behavior of emission peaks in N-GQDs observed, a deep insight into the optical property of GQDs is relatively necessary for understanding their luminescence. The optimum concentrations of A-GQDs and U-GQDs are 0.5 mg/mL, and the intensities of the corresponding emission peak are strong. However, the PL intensity and emission peak (or fluorescence color) varies at the same concentration for different reaction systems. The surface bonding structures of N-GQDs have much influence on their electronic structures, electron–hole recombination ways, and fluorescence properties. For A-GQDs, multiple C- and N-containing functional groups are found on the surface of the produced GQDs, resulting in various surface states with different energy levels and a series of emissive traps. The PL intensity and emission peak of A-GQDs is greatly affected by the size and the surface oxygen functional group. The optical property of U-GQDs is resembled to that of crystalline GQDs. Thus, the PL mainly comes from the uniform distribution and high crystalline structure.

In this study, various N-GQDs are designed and synthesized to investigate the N-doping process under hydrothermal conditions. CA is chosen as carbon source because it is easily dehydrated between molecules to form a graphene skeleton. The N atoms, which are dehydrated inside the molecule, enter the graphene skeleton to form a pyrrolic structure. More N atoms go into the graphene skeleton with continuous reaction, and the N-containing doped rings of A-GQDs are dominated by five-membered rings, and U-GQDs are mostly seven-membered rings at the same time. As the carbonization progresses, the structural instability of the nitrogen-containing doped ring itself, mostly forms a stable nitrogen-containing ring structure, which may be pyrrolic nitrogen or graphitized nitrogen. The diversities in N doping and carbonization affect the PL properties and QY of N-GQDs. The QY of A-GQDs and U-GQDs reaches as high as 45.4 % and 54 %, respectively, by optimizing the reaction conditions. A-GQDs exhibit such excitation-dependent PL behavior related to the surface states. On the contrary, U-GQDs show high PL intensity and relatively uniform PL emission behavior. Accordingly, the mechanism and luminescent properties of N-GQDs can be better investigated using a simple and inspiring synthesis method. Future research and application of N-GQDs will focus on ion detection and photovoltaic devices.

Yanyun Zhu: Methodology, Validation, Writing - original draft. Luting Yan: Supervision, Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing. Mingyuan Xu: Formal analysis. Yuting Li: Investigation, Resources. Xiaodong Song: Data curation, Resources. Lan Yin: Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 11475017).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfa.2020.125703.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Glowing graphene quantum dots and carbon dots: properties, syntheses, and biological applications
Small

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tuning the electronic and optical properties of graphene quantum dots by selective boronization
J. Mater. Chem. C

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Feng, H. Dong, B. Pang, Y. Chen, L. Yu, L. Dong, Tuning the electronic and optical properties of graphene quantum dots by selective boronization, J. Mater. Chem. C. 7 (2019) 237–246. https://doi.org/10.1039/c8tc03863e.
